topics.name: Why Python?

Need for Python: Python was created in 1991 by Guido van Rossum. The goal was simple: write code that reads like plain English, so programmers spend less time debugging syntax and more time solving problems.
Today Python powers data science (Pandas, NumPy), web development (Django, Flask), automation, AI, and machine learning. It is the #1 most popular language worldwide (TIOBE Index 2024).
Real-world use: Instagram backend runs on Python/Django. NASA uses Python for mission analysis. Google, YouTube, Netflix all use Python heavily.
Advantages and Disadvantages: Advantages: easy to read and write (English-like syntax), huge library ecosystem (pip install anything), cross-platform (same code runs on Windows/Mac/Linux), large community support.
Disadvantages: slower than C/C++ (interpreted language), high memory usage (not ideal for embedded systems), Global Interpreter Lock (GIL) limits true multi-threading.
When to choose Python: data analysis, automation, rapid prototyping, machine learning. When NOT to choose: game engines needing raw speed, mobile apps, systems programming.

required_tables: Comparison: Feature | Python | C++ || Syntax | Simple, English-like | Complex, verbose || Speed | Slower (interpreted) | Faster (compiled) || Use case | Data science, AI, web | Systems, games, embedded || Learning curve | Easy | Steep || Community | Huge | Large
common_mistakes: Thinking Python is too slow for everything — Python is fast enough for 90% of applications. Speed matters only for compute-intensive tasks like game engines

topics.name: Setting Up Python

Downloading and Installing Python: Download from python.org → choose latest stable version (3.x) → install with 'Add Python to PATH' checkbox ticked.
Verify installation: open terminal → type python --version → should show Python 3.x.x. pip comes bundled — use pip install package_name to add libraries.
Compilation vs Interpretation: Compiled languages (C, C++): source code → compiler → machine code (exe) → runs directly on CPU. Fast execution but platform-specific.
Python is interpreted: source code (.py) → Python interpreter reads and executes line by line. Internally creates bytecode (.pyc) for efficiency. Slower but more flexible.
Python actually does both: source → bytecode (.pyc, stored in __pycache__) → Python Virtual Machine (PVM) executes bytecode. This is why Python is sometimes called 'interpreted'.
Script Mode vs Interactive Mode: Interactive mode: type python in terminal → Python prompt (>>>) appears → type code and see result immediately. Great for testing small ideas.
Script mode: write code in .py file → run with python filename.py → entire file executes. Used for real programs. Jupyter Notebook combines both — cells run interactively.
Command Line Arguments: Pass values to script when running it: python script.py Alice 25. Inside script, access via sys.argv — sys.argv[0] is script name, sys.argv[1] is 'Alice', sys.argv[2] is '25'.
import sys then use sys.argv list. Useful for automation scripts where you don't want to hardcode values.
Important IDEs for Python: IDLE: comes bundled with Python, simple, good for beginners. VS Code: free, powerful, best for professional development (install Python extension).
PyCharm: dedicated Python IDE, excellent debugging and autocomplete. Jupyter Notebook: best for data science and learning — write code in cells, see output immediately.

topics.required_code:
# Interactive mode — type in terminal
# >>> 2 + 3
# 5
# >>> print('Hello')
# Hello

# Script mode — save as hello.py and run with: python hello.py
print('Hello, World!')

# Command line arguments
import sys
print('Script name:', sys.argv[0])
print('First argument:', sys.argv[1])  # python script.py Alice
# Output: First argument: Alice
common_mistakes: Forgetting to tick 'Add Python to PATH' during installation — python command won't work in terminal without it
Confusing Python 2 and Python 3 — always use Python 3. Python 2 is dead (end of life 2020)

topics.name: Python Syntax and Tokens

Keywords: Keywords: reserved words with special meaning in Python. Cannot use as variable names. Examples: if, else, for, while, def, class, return, import, True, False, None, and, or, not.
Python has 35 keywords. Check all with: import keyword; print(keyword.kwlist). Case-sensitive — 'True' is a keyword, 'true' is not.
Identifiers: Identifiers: names you give to variables, functions, classes. Rules: start with letter or underscore, can contain letters/digits/underscore, case-sensitive, cannot be a keyword.
Good naming: student_name (snake_case for variables), calculateArea (camelCase), MAX_SIZE (UPPER_CASE for constants). Bad: 1name (starts with digit), my-var (hyphen not allowed).
Literals: Literals: fixed values written directly in code. Types: Integer (42), Float (3.14), String ('hello'), Boolean (True/False), None (represents absence of value).
String literals: single quotes ('hello'), double quotes ("hello"), triple quotes ('''multi-line'''). All are valid. f-strings: f'Hello {name}' — embed expressions inside strings.
Punctuators: Punctuators: special symbols with meaning. Colon (:) after if/for/def/class. Comma (,) separates items. Parentheses () for function calls/tuples. Square brackets [] for lists/indexing. Curly braces {} for dicts/sets.
Indentation is Python's block separator — NOT curly braces like C/Java. 4 spaces (or 1 tab) per level. Inconsistent indentation causes IndentationError.

topics.required_code:
# Keywords example
import keyword
print(keyword.kwlist)  # shows all 35 keywords

# Valid identifiers
student_name = 'Alice'   # good
_private_var = 10        # valid (leading underscore = convention for private)
age2 = 25                # valid (digit not at start)

# Literals
x = 42           # integer literal
y = 3.14         # float literal
name = 'Python'  # string literal
is_valid = True  # boolean literal
result = None    # None literal

# f-string (most modern way)
print(f'Hello {name}, version {x}')  # Hello Python, version 42
common_mistakes: Using keywords as variable names — if = 5 gives SyntaxError. Use descriptive names like condition or flag instead
Mixing tabs and spaces for indentation — Python 3 raises TabError. Always use spaces (4 per level)

topics.name: Variables and Data Types

Variables and Constants: Variable: a name that points to a value in memory. Python is dynamically typed — no need to declare type. x = 5 makes x point to integer 5.
Multiple assignment: x, y, z = 1, 2, 3. Swap without temp: x, y = y, x. Python has no true constants — convention is UPPER_CASE for values that shouldn't change: PI = 3.14159.
type() function tells you what a variable holds: type(42) → int, type(3.14) → float, type('hi') → str.
Input and Output Operations: print(): outputs to screen. print('Hello') → Hello. print('a', 'b', sep='-') → a-b. print('Hello', end='') removes newline.
input(): reads user input as STRING always. age = input('Enter age: '). To use as number: age = int(input('Enter age: ')). Forgetting int() conversion is most common beginner error.
Operators in Python: Arithmetic: + - * / // % **. Important: / always returns float (7/2 = 3.5). // is floor division (7//2 = 3). % is modulo/remainder (7%2 = 1). ** is power (2**8 = 256).
Comparison: == != > < >= <=. Returns True or False. Logical: and, or, not. Assignment: = += -= *= /=.
Walrus operator := (Python 3.8+): assigns and returns value in one step. if (n := len(data)) > 10: print(n). Useful in while loops.

topics.required_code:
# Variables
name = 'Alice'
age = 20
gpa = 8.5
print(type(name), type(age), type(gpa))  # str int float

# Multiple assignment
x, y = 10, 20
x, y = y, x  # swap
print(x, y)  # 20 10

# Input/Output
name = input('Enter your name: ')      # always string
age  = int(input('Enter your age: '))  # convert to int
print(f'Hello {name}, you are {age} years old')

# Operators
print(7 / 2)   # 3.5  (true division)
print(7 // 2)  # 3    (floor division)
print(7 % 2)   # 1    (remainder)
print(2 ** 8)  # 256  (power)
common_mistakes: Using = instead of == for comparison — if x = 5 is SyntaxError in Python (assignment not allowed in condition without walrus operator)
Forgetting to convert input() — age = input() + 1 gives TypeError because input returns string

topics.name: Data Types
subtopics: Basic Data Types (Integer, Float, Complex, String, Boolean)
Advanced Data Types (List, Tuple, Set, Dictionary)

Basic Data Types (Integer, Float, Comple: int: whole numbers of any size — 42, -7, 0. Python integers have unlimited precision (no overflow).
float: decimal numbers — 3.14, -0.5. Stored as 64-bit floating point. Careful: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004 (floating point imprecision).
complex: numbers with real and imaginary parts — 3+4j. bool: subclass of int — True=1, False=0. True+True = 2.
Advanced Data Types (List, Tuple, Set, D: List []: ordered, mutable, allows duplicates. fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'apple']. Can change elements.
Tuple (): ordered, immutable, allows duplicates. point = (3, 4). Cannot change after creation — use for fixed data like coordinates.
Set {}: unordered, no duplicates, mutable. {1, 2, 3, 2} → {1, 2, 3}. Perfect for removing duplicates. Dict {key:value}: unordered (Python 3.7+ maintains insertion order), keys unique.
Mutable vs Immutable Types: Immutable: once created, value cannot change. int, float, str, tuple, bool. When you do x = 5; x = 6, you're not changing 5, you're making x point to 6.
Mutable: can be modified in place. list, dict, set. If a = [1,2,3] and b = a, then b[0] = 99 also changes a — they point to same object. Use copy() to avoid this.
Type Casting (Implicit and Explicit): Implicit conversion: Python automatically converts when safe. int + float → float. 3 + 3.0 = 6.0. No data loss.
Explicit conversion: you manually convert using int(), float(), str(), list(), tuple(), set(). int('42') → 42. int(3.9) → 3 (truncates, not rounds). str(42) → '42'.

topics.required_code:
# Basic types
x = 42          # int
y = 3.14        # float
z = 3+4j        # complex
s = 'hello'     # str
b = True        # bool

# Advanced types
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']  # list — mutable
point  = (10, 20)                        # tuple — immutable
unique = {1, 2, 3, 2, 1}                # set — no duplicates → {1,2,3}
student = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 20}  # dict

# Mutability
fruits[0] = 'mango'  # OK — list is mutable
# point[0] = 5       # TypeError — tuple is immutable

# Type casting
print(int('42'))     # 42
print(float('3.14')) # 3.14
print(str(100))      # '100'
print(int(3.9))      # 3 (not 4 — truncates!)
required_tables: Comparison: Type | Example | Ordered | Mutable | Duplicates || List | [1,2,3] | Yes | Yes | Yes || Tuple | (1,2,3) | Yes | No | Yes || Set | {1,2,3} | No | Yes | No || Dict | {'a':1} | Yes(3.7+) | Yes | Keys: No
common_mistakes: int(3.9) gives 3, not 4 — int() truncates towards zero, it does NOT round. Use round() for rounding
Thinking a = b creates a copy for lists — both variables point to same list. Use b = a.copy() for independent copy

topics.name: Understanding Flow Control

Flowcharts: Flowchart: visual diagram showing step-by-step flow of a program. Symbols: oval (start/end), rectangle (process/action), diamond (decision/condition), arrow (flow direction).
Use flowcharts before coding to plan your logic. A flowchart for login: Start → Input username/password → Correct? → Yes: Go to dashboard / No: Show error → End.
Pseudocode: Pseudocode: writing program logic in plain English, not actual code. No strict syntax — just describe what the program should do step by step.
Example pseudocode for finding max: START. Input num1, num2. IF num1 > num2 THEN print num1 ELSE print num2. END. Pseudocode helps think through logic before worrying about syntax.

required_diagrams: Simple flowchart diagram: show a grade-checking flowchart. Start oval → Rectangle (Input marks) → Diamond (marks >= 60?) → Yes arrow to Rectangle (Print Pass) → No arrow to Rectangle (Print Fail) → both connect to End oval. Standard flowchart symbols, clean white background.
common_mistakes: Skipping planning and directly writing code — always plan with flowchart/pseudocode for complex problems to avoid logical errors

topics.name: Conditional Statements

If, Else, Elif: if: executes block only if condition is True. else: executes when if condition is False. elif: check another condition if previous ones were False.
Important: indentation defines the block — all lines indented under if belong to that if block. Colon (:) is mandatory after condition.
Ternary (one-line if-else): result = 'Pass' if marks >= 60 else 'Fail'. Useful for simple conditions — avoid for complex logic.

common_mistakes: Using = instead of == in condition — if x = 5 is SyntaxError. Always use == for comparison
Forgetting colon (:) after if/elif/else — causes SyntaxError

topics.name: Loops in Python

For Loop: for loop: iterates over a sequence (list, string, range, tuple). Loop variable takes each value one by one. Most used loop in Python.
for item in collection: body. Loop variable name is your choice. Loop runs once per item — automatically stops when sequence ends.
While Loop: while loop: runs as long as condition is True. You control when it stops. Used when you don't know in advance how many times to loop.
Always make sure the condition eventually becomes False — otherwise infinite loop. while True: with break inside is a common pattern for menu-driven programs.
Nested Loops: Nested loop: loop inside another loop. Outer loop runs N times; for each iteration, inner loop runs M times. Total iterations = N × M.
Common use: printing patterns, working with 2D data (matrices/tables). For 3 rows and 4 columns: outer for row in range(3), inner for col in range(4).
Range Function: range(stop): generates 0 to stop-1. range(5) → 0,1,2,3,4. range(start, stop): start to stop-1. range(2,6) → 2,3,4,5.
range(start, stop, step): range(0,10,2) → 0,2,4,6,8. Negative step for countdown: range(5,0,-1) → 5,4,3,2,1. range doesn't create a list — it generates numbers one by one (memory efficient).

topics.required_code:
# For loop with list
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
for fruit in fruits:
    print(fruit)

# For loop with range
for i in range(5):        # 0,1,2,3,4
    print(i, end=' ')     # 0 1 2 3 4

# While loop
count = 1
while count <= 5:
    print(count, end=' ')  # 1 2 3 4 5
    count += 1             # MUST increment or infinite loop!

# Nested loop — multiplication table
for i in range(1, 4):
    for j in range(1, 4):
        print(i * j, end='\t')
    print()  # new line after each row
# 1  2  3
# 2  4  6
# 3  6  9
common_mistakes: Forgetting to increment in while loop — while count <= 5: print(count) without count += 1 runs forever
range(5) gives 0-4, NOT 1-5 — common off-by-one error. Use range(1,6) for 1 to 5

topics.name: Jump Statements

Break: break: immediately exits the loop, skipping remaining iterations. Used when you found what you were looking for.
Example: searching a list — once found, no need to check remaining items. break only exits the innermost loop it's in.
Continue: continue: skips the rest of current iteration and jumps to next. Does NOT exit loop — continues with next item.
Example: skip even numbers, print only odd. or skip empty values in data processing.
Pass: pass: does nothing — it's a placeholder. Used when Python syntax requires a statement but you have nothing to write yet.
Common uses: empty function body (def func(): pass), empty class (class MyClass: pass), empty if block during development. Without pass, empty blocks cause SyntaxError.

common_mistakes: Confusing break and continue — break EXITS loop, continue SKIPS current iteration and continues loop
Using pass thinking it's like a comment — pass is a statement that does nothing but satisfies Python's syntax requirement

topics.name: Introduction to Functions

What is a function?: Function: a named block of code that performs a specific task. Define once, call many times. Syntax: def function_name(parameters): body.
Two steps: define (write the function using def) and call (use the function by its name). Calling a function runs its code.
Need for functions: Without functions: same code written 10 places = 10 places to fix when bug found. With functions: fix once, fixed everywhere.
Functions enable: code reuse (DRY — Don't Repeat Yourself), modularity (break big problem into small pieces), readability (function name explains what it does), easier testing.

topics.required_code:
# Without function — repetitive
print('Welcome, Alice!')
print('Welcome, Bob!')
print('Welcome, Charlie!')

# With function — reusable
def greet(name):
    print(f'Welcome, {name}!')

greet('Alice')    # Welcome, Alice!
greet('Bob')      # Welcome, Bob!
greet('Charlie')  # Welcome, Charlie!

# Function with return value
def square(n):
    return n * n

result = square(5)
print(result)  # 25
common_mistakes: Defining function but forgetting to call it — the def block only defines, you must write greet('Alice') to actually run it
Printing inside function instead of returning — prefer return so the caller can use the result however they want

topics.name: Types of Functions

User-Defined Functions: User-defined: functions you write yourself using def. Can have parameters (inputs) and return values (outputs). return exits function and sends value back.
If no return statement, function returns None automatically. Multiple return values: def min_max(lst): return min(lst), max(lst) → returns a tuple.
Built-in Functions: Python has 68+ built-in functions — ready to use without importing anything. Common: print(), input(), len(), range(), type(), int(), str(), float(), list(), sum(), min(), max(), abs(), round(), sorted(), enumerate(), zip().
dir(__builtins__) shows all built-in names. help(function_name) shows documentation. These are optimized in C — faster than equivalent Python code.
Lambda Functions (Filter, Reduce, Map): Lambda: anonymous one-line function. Syntax: lambda parameters: expression. square = lambda x: x*x. Use when function is simple and used only once.
map(function, iterable): applies function to every item. list(map(lambda x: x*2, [1,2,3])) → [2,4,6].
filter(function, iterable): keeps items where function returns True. list(filter(lambda x: x>3, [1,2,3,4,5])) → [4,5].
reduce(function, iterable): combines all items into single value. from functools import reduce. reduce(lambda x,y: x+y, [1,2,3,4]) → 10 (1+2+3+4).
Recursive Functions: Recursive function: calls itself to solve a smaller version of the same problem. Must have: base case (stops recursion) and recursive case (calls itself with simpler input).
Factorial example: factorial(5) = 5 × factorial(4) = 5 × 4 × factorial(3) = ... = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120. Base case: factorial(0) = 1.
Python's default recursion limit is 1000 calls (sys.setrecursionlimit() to change). For large problems, iteration (loop) is usually better than recursion.

topics.required_code:
# User-defined function
def calculate_area(length, width):
    area = length * width
    return area

print(calculate_area(5, 3))  # 15

# Built-in functions
numbers = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9]
print(len(numbers))    # 6
print(sum(numbers))    # 23
print(max(numbers))    # 9
print(sorted(numbers)) # [1,1,3,4,5,9]

# Lambda with map, filter, reduce
from functools import reduce
nums = [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
doubled  = list(map(lambda x: x*2, nums))      # [2,4,6,8,10]
evens    = list(filter(lambda x: x%2==0, nums)) # [2,4]
total    = reduce(lambda x,y: x+y, nums)         # 15

# Recursive factorial
def factorial(n):
    if n == 0:                  # base case
        return 1
    return n * factorial(n-1)   # recursive case

print(factorial(5))  # 120
common_mistakes: Forgetting base case in recursion — without it, function calls itself forever until Python raises RecursionError
Lambda for complex logic — lambda is for simple one-liners. Use regular def for multi-line functions

topics.name: Function Arguments

Positional Arguments: Positional: values matched to parameters by position (order matters). greet('Alice', 20) → name='Alice', age=20. Most common type.
Default Arguments: Default: parameter has a default value used when argument not provided. def greet(name, msg='Hello'): — if msg not passed, uses 'Hello'.
Rule: default arguments must come AFTER non-default arguments. def func(a, b=5): OK. def func(a=5, b): SyntaxError.
Keyword Arguments: Keyword: pass arguments by name, so order doesn't matter. greet(age=20, name='Alice') — same as greet('Alice', 20).
Mixing: positional arguments must come before keyword arguments. greet('Alice', age=20) is OK. greet(name='Alice', 20) is SyntaxError.
Variable-Length Arguments: *args: collects extra positional arguments into a tuple. def add(*nums): — can call with add(1,2,3) or add(1,2,3,4,5).
Inside function, args is a tuple. Iterate with for num in nums: sum += num. Use when you don't know how many arguments will be passed.
Variable-Length Keyword Arguments: **kwargs: collects extra keyword arguments into a dictionary. def show(**info): — can call with show(name='Alice', age=20, city='Delhi').
Inside function, kwargs is a dict. Access with kwargs['name'] or iterate with for key, value in kwargs.items().

topics.required_code:
# Positional
def greet(name, age):
    print(f'{name} is {age} years old')
greet('Alice', 20)  # Alice is 20 years old

# Default
def power(base, exp=2):
    return base ** exp
print(power(3))    # 9  (3^2 — default exp=2)
print(power(3, 3)) # 27 (3^3)

# Keyword
greet(age=20, name='Alice')  # order doesn't matter

# *args — variable positional
def total(*numbers):
    return sum(numbers)
print(total(1, 2, 3))       # 6
print(total(1, 2, 3, 4, 5)) # 15

# **kwargs — variable keyword
def show_info(**details):
    for key, value in details.items():
        print(f'{key}: {value}')
show_info(name='Alice', age=20, city='Delhi')
common_mistakes: Default arguments after non-default — def func(a=5, b): gives SyntaxError. Always put defaults last
Mutable default argument — def func(lst=[]): is dangerous! Default list shared across calls. Use def func(lst=None): then lst = lst or [] inside

topics.name: Function Documentation

Docstrings: Docstring: string literal as first statement of function/class/module. Documents what the function does, its parameters, and return value.
Access with help(function_name) or function_name.__doc__. Good practice: always write docstrings for functions others will use.
Format: single-line for simple functions, multi-line for complex. Multi-line starts and ends with triple quotes. Describes: purpose, Args (parameters), Returns.

topics.required_code:
def calculate_bmi(weight, height):
    """
    Calculate Body Mass Index (BMI).
    
    Args:
        weight (float): Weight in kilograms
        height (float): Height in meters
    
    Returns:
        float: BMI value rounded to 2 decimal places
    
    Example:
        >>> calculate_bmi(70, 1.75)
        22.86
    """
    bmi = weight / (height ** 2)
    return round(bmi, 2)

# Access documentation
help(calculate_bmi)         # shows full docstring
print(calculate_bmi.__doc__) # prints docstring directly
print(calculate_bmi(70, 1.75))  # 22.86
common_mistakes: Skipping docstrings thinking code is self-explanatory — 6 months later even you won't remember what the function does without documentation

topics.name: Creating and Accessing Lists

Creating lists: List: ordered, mutable collection. Created with square brackets []. Can hold mixed types. Empty list: []. list() constructor: list((1,2,3)) converts tuple to list.
Nested list: [[1,2],[3,4],[5,6]] — list of lists. Used for 2D data like matrices or tables.
Accessing list elements: Positive indexing: starts from 0. fruits[0] = first item, fruits[1] = second. Negative indexing: fruits[-1] = last item, fruits[-2] = second from last.
Index out of range: accessing fruits[10] when list has 5 items gives IndexError. Check length with len(fruits) before accessing.
Traversing a list: Method 1 — for item in list: print(item). Simple, Pythonic. Method 2 — for i in range(len(list)): use when you need the index.
enumerate() gives both index and value: for i, item in enumerate(fruits): print(i, item). Best of both worlds.
List slicing: Slicing: extract a portion of list. list[start:stop:step]. Stop index is exclusive (not included).
fruits[1:3] → items at index 1 and 2. fruits[:3] → first 3 items. fruits[2:] → from index 2 to end. fruits[::-1] → reversed list. fruits[::2] → every second item.
List replication: * operator repeats list. [0] * 5 → [0,0,0,0,0]. Useful to create list with default values.
Caution: [[]]*3 creates [[],[],[]] where all three inner lists are the SAME object. Modifying one modifies all. Use [[] for _ in range(3)] instead for independent lists.

topics.required_code:
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry', 'date']

# Accessing
print(fruits[0])   # apple (first)
print(fruits[-1])  # date (last)

# Traversing with enumerate
for i, fruit in enumerate(fruits):
    print(i, fruit)  # 0 apple, 1 banana...

# Slicing
print(fruits[1:3])   # ['banana', 'cherry']
print(fruits[:2])    # ['apple', 'banana']
print(fruits[::2])   # ['apple', 'cherry'] (every 2nd)
print(fruits[::-1])  # ['date','cherry','banana','apple'] (reversed)

# Replication
zeros = [0] * 5
print(zeros)  # [0, 0, 0, 0, 0]
common_mistakes: IndexError from off-by-one — list has 4 items, valid indices are 0-3. fruits[4] gives IndexError
Slicing fruits[1:3] gives items at index 1 and 2 only — stop index (3) is NOT included

topics.name: Modifying Lists

Appending Two Lists (Append, Extend, + o: append(item): adds ONE item to end of list. fruits.append('mango') adds mango as single element.
extend(iterable): adds ALL items of another list to end. fruits.extend(['kiwi','grape']) adds both. + operator creates NEW list: fruits + ['kiwi'] doesn't modify fruits.
insert(index, item): inserts at specific position. fruits.insert(1, 'mango') puts mango at index 1, shifts others right.
Removing an Element (Pop, Delete): pop(): removes and returns last item. pop(index): removes and returns item at index. Useful when you need the removed value.
del list[index]: deletes item at index. del list[1:3]: deletes a slice. remove(value): finds and deletes first occurrence of value (not index).
clear(): removes all items, list becomes empty []. Difference: del list removes the variable itself, list.clear() keeps the variable but empties it.

topics.required_code:
nums = [1, 2, 3]

# Adding
nums.append(4)          # [1,2,3,4]
nums.extend([5, 6])     # [1,2,3,4,5,6]
nums.insert(0, 0)       # [0,1,2,3,4,5,6]
new = nums + [7, 8]     # creates new list, nums unchanged

# Removing
print(nums.pop())    # removes & returns 6 (last)
print(nums.pop(0))   # removes & returns 0 (first)
del nums[1]          # removes item at index 1
nums.remove(3)       # removes first occurrence of value 3
print(nums)          # [1, 2, 4, 5]
common_mistakes: append([1,2]) adds the LIST as one item — list becomes [[1,2]]. Use extend([1,2]) to add items individually
remove() raises ValueError if value not in list. Check with if value in list: before removing

topics.name: List Copying

Reference Type Assignment: b = a does NOT copy the list — both variables point to same list in memory. Changing b changes a too.
To verify: a = [1,2,3]; b = a; print(a is b) → True (same object). This surprises most beginners.
Shallow Copy: Shallow copy: creates new list with same elements. Methods: b = a.copy(), b = a[:], b = list(a).
For simple (non-nested) lists, shallow copy works perfectly. Problem: for nested lists [[1,2],[3,4]], inner lists are still shared references.
Deep Copy: Deep copy: completely independent copy — new list AND new copies of all nested objects. Use copy.deepcopy().
import copy; b = copy.deepcopy(a). Now modifying b at any level does NOT affect a. Use whenever list contains other lists (or dicts/objects).

topics.required_code:
import copy

# Reference — same object
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a           # b points to same list
b[0] = 99
print(a)        # [99, 2, 3] — a changed too!

# Shallow copy — works for simple lists
a = [1, 2, 3]
b = a.copy()    # or b = a[:]
b[0] = 99
print(a)        # [1, 2, 3] — a unchanged

# Shallow copy problem with nested list
a = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
b = a.copy()
b[0][0] = 99
print(a)        # [[99, 2], [3, 4]] — inner list still shared!

# Deep copy — fully independent
a = [[1, 2], [3, 4]]
b = copy.deepcopy(a)
b[0][0] = 99
print(a)        # [[1, 2], [3, 4]] — unchanged
common_mistakes: Using = to 'copy' a list — this creates a reference, not a copy. Always use .copy() or copy.deepcopy()
Thinking shallow copy is always enough — it fails for nested lists. Use deepcopy when list contains other lists

topics.name: List Comprehensions

Using For Loop: List comprehension: create list in one line using [expression for item in iterable]. Shorter and faster than equivalent for loop.
squares = [x**2 for x in range(1,6)] → [1,4,9,16,25]. Readable once you get used to the pattern: think 'give me x**2 for each x in range(1,6)'.
Single If Condition: Filter with condition: [expression for item in iterable if condition]. Only includes items where condition is True.
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x%2==0] → [0,2,4,6,8].
Multiple If Conditions: Multiple conditions: [x for x in range(50) if x%2==0 if x%3==0] → numbers divisible by both 2 and 3.
Equivalent to: if x%2==0 and x%3==0. Multiple if conditions act like AND.
If-Else Condition: If-else in comprehension: [expression_if_true if condition else expression_if_false for item in iterable].
labels = ['even' if x%2==0 else 'odd' for x in range(5)] → ['even','odd','even','odd','even'].

topics.required_code:
# Basic comprehension
squares = [x**2 for x in range(1, 6)]
print(squares)  # [1, 4, 9, 16, 25]

# With single condition (filter)
evens = [x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0]
print(evens)    # [0, 2, 4, 6, 8]

# Multiple conditions
divisible = [x for x in range(50) if x%2==0 if x%3==0]
print(divisible)  # [0, 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36, 42, 48]

# If-else (ternary in comprehension)
labels = ['even' if x%2==0 else 'odd' for x in range(6)]
print(labels)   # ['even', 'odd', 'even', 'odd', 'even', 'odd']

# Nested comprehension (flatten 2D list)
matrix = [[1,2,3],[4,5,6],[7,8,9]]
flat = [num for row in matrix for num in row]
print(flat)     # [1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8,9]
common_mistakes: Confusing if-else position — [x if cond else y for x in lst] (if-else before for). [x for x in lst if cond] (filter after for). Different positions, different meanings

topics.name: List Operations

Reversing a List: list.reverse(): reverses IN PLACE — modifies original list. Returns None. reversed(list): returns iterator, doesn't modify original.
list[::-1]: creates new reversed list (slicing). Most commonly used when you need reversed copy without modifying original.
List Comparison: Lists compared element by element. [1,2,3] == [1,2,3] → True. [1,2,3] == [1,2,4] → False.
[1,2] < [1,3] → True (compare element by element, first difference decides). [1,2,3] < [1,2,3,4] → True (shorter list is 'less').
Sorting (Ascending, Descending): list.sort(): sorts IN PLACE — modifies original. sorted(list): returns new sorted list, original unchanged.
Descending: list.sort(reverse=True) or sorted(list, reverse=True). Custom sort: list.sort(key=len) — sort strings by length.
Membership Check in Lists: in operator: 'apple' in fruits → True/False. not in: 'mango' not in fruits → True/False.
O(n) time — checks every element. For frequent membership checks on large data, use set instead (O(1) lookup).

topics.required_code:
nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5, 9, 2, 6]

# Reverse
nums.reverse()            # modifies in place
print(nums)               # [6, 2, 9, 5, 1, 4, 1, 3]
reversed_copy = nums[::-1]  # new list, original unchanged

# Sort
nums.sort()               # ascending, in place
print(nums)               # [1, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 9]
nums.sort(reverse=True)   # descending
print(nums)               # [9, 6, 5, 4, 3, 2, 1, 1]

# sorted() — original unchanged
original = [3, 1, 4]
new_sorted = sorted(original)
print(original)   # [3, 1, 4] — unchanged
print(new_sorted) # [1, 3, 4]

# Membership
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
print('apple' in fruits)    # True
print('mango' in fruits)    # False
print('mango' not in fruits) # True
common_mistakes: result = list.sort() — sort() returns None, not the sorted list. Use sorted(list) if you need the return value
sorted() vs sort() — sorted() creates new list (original safe), sort() modifies original (original changed)

topics.name: Creating and Accessing Tuples

Creating tuples: Tuple: ordered, immutable sequence. Created with parentheses () or just commas. t = (1, 2, 3) or t = 1, 2, 3 — both create tuple.
Empty tuple: () or tuple(). tuple() converts other iterables: tuple([1,2,3]) → (1,2,3). Mixed types allowed: (1, 'hello', 3.14, True).
Singleton Tuples: Single-element tuple: MUST have trailing comma. t = (5,) is a tuple. t = (5) is just the integer 5 in parentheses — NOT a tuple.
Verify: type((5,)) → tuple. type((5)) → int. The comma is what makes it a tuple, not the parentheses.
Accessing elements: Same as lists: indexing (t[0]), negative indexing (t[-1]), slicing (t[1:3]). Since tuples are immutable, you can read but NOT modify.
t[0] = 10 raises TypeError: 'tuple' object does not support item assignment.
Packing and Unpacking: Packing: combining values into tuple. t = 1, 2, 3 — packs three values.
Unpacking: assigning tuple elements to variables. a, b, c = (1, 2, 3). Number of variables must match number of elements (or use * for rest).
Extended unpacking: first, *rest = (1,2,3,4,5) → first=1, rest=[2,3,4,5]. *middle, last = (1,2,3,4,5) → middle=[1,2,3,4], last=5.

topics.required_code:
# Creating tuples
t1 = (1, 2, 3)
t2 = 1, 2, 3           # no parentheses — still a tuple
t3 = ()                 # empty tuple
t4 = (5,)               # singleton — comma required!
print(type((5)))  # int
print(type((5,))) # tuple

# Accessing
coords = (10, 20, 30)
print(coords[0])    # 10
print(coords[-1])   # 30
print(coords[1:3])  # (20, 30)

# Packing and Unpacking
point = 3, 4          # packing
x, y = point          # unpacking
print(x, y)           # 3 4

# Extended unpacking
first, *rest = (1, 2, 3, 4, 5)
print(first)  # 1
print(rest)   # [2, 3, 4, 5]
common_mistakes: t = (5) is not a tuple — it's just integer 5 in parentheses. Must write t = (5,) with trailing comma
Trying to modify tuple — t[0] = 10 raises TypeError. Tuples are immutable by design

topics.name: Tuple Operations and Comparison

Unpacking with Disposable Variable: Use _ (underscore) for values you want to ignore during unpacking. Convention: _ means 'I don't care about this value'.
first, _, third = (1, 2, 3) — ignores middle value. first, *_ = (1,2,3,4,5) — only care about first.
Tuple Slicing: Slicing creates a new tuple. t[1:3] returns tuple of items at index 1 and 2. t[::-1] returns reversed tuple.
Slicing a tuple is safe — no mutation possible.
Copy Operations in Tuples: Since tuples are immutable, 'copying' is simpler — b = a gives same tuple, but since tuples can't be modified, no accidental mutation.
tuple(a) creates new tuple object from existing. For tuples containing mutable objects (like lists), deepcopy still applies.
List vs Tuple: Use tuple when: data should not change (coordinates, RGB colors, database records), function returning multiple values, dictionary keys (lists can't be dict keys).
Use list when: data will be modified (adding/removing items), need list-specific methods (sort, append, pop).
Tuples are faster than lists and use less memory — for fixed data, prefer tuple.

topics.required_code:
# Disposable variable
first, _, last = (1, 99, 3)  # ignore middle
print(first, last)             # 1 3

# Tuple slicing
t = (10, 20, 30, 40, 50)
print(t[1:4])   # (20, 30, 40)
print(t[::-1])  # (50, 40, 30, 20, 10)

# Tuples as dict keys (lists can't)
coord_map = {(0,0): 'origin', (1,0): 'east'}
print(coord_map[(0,0)])  # origin

# list_key = {[0,0]: 'x'}  # TypeError — list unhashable
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | List | Tuple || Syntax | [] | () || Mutable | Yes | No || Speed | Slower | Faster || Memory | More | Less || Dict key | No | Yes || Use case | Dynamic data | Fixed data, return values
common_mistakes: Using list when tuple is appropriate — returning multiple values from function should use tuple, not list

topics.name: Creating and Set Operations

Creating Sets: Set: unordered collection of unique elements. Created with curly braces {} or set(). No duplicates — {1,2,2,3} → {1,2,3} automatically.
CAUTION: empty set must be set(), NOT {} — {} creates empty dictionary. set() creates empty set.
Accessing Elements: Sets are unordered — no indexing, no slicing. Cannot do s[0]. Iterate with for x in my_set.
Check membership with in: 3 in my_set → True/False. This is O(1) — much faster than list membership check.
Union: Union: all elements from both sets (no duplicates). A | B or A.union(B). Like 'OR' in logic.
Intersection: Intersection: only elements present in BOTH sets. A & B or A.intersection(B). Like 'AND' in logic.
Difference: Difference: elements in A but NOT in B. A - B or A.difference(B). Order matters — A-B ≠ B-A.
Symmetric Difference: Symmetric difference: elements in either A or B but NOT in both. A ^ B or A.symmetric_difference(B).
Subset and Superset: Subset: A is subset of B if all elements of A are in B. A <= B or A.issubset(B). {1,2} <= {1,2,3} → True.
Superset: A is superset of B if A contains all elements of B. A >= B or A.issuperset(B). {1,2,3} >= {1,2} → True.
Add, Discard, Remove: add(x): adds element. If already present, no change. No error.
remove(x): removes element. Raises KeyError if not present. discard(x): removes if present, NO error if not present. Prefer discard() for safety.

common_mistakes: Using {} for empty set — {} creates empty dict. Must use set() for empty set
Using remove() when element might not exist — use discard() to avoid KeyError

topics.name: Set Comprehension and Advanced Concepts

Using For Loop: Set comprehension: {expression for item in iterable}. Like list comprehension but with {} and produces a set (unique elements).
squares = {x**2 for x in range(-3,4)} → {0,1,4,9} (no duplicates even though -3 and 3 both give 9).
Single If Condition: evens = {x for x in range(10) if x%2==0} → {0,2,4,6,8}.
If-Else Condition: {x if x>0 else -x for x in [-3,-1,0,1,3]} → {0,1,3} — absolute values (as set, no duplicates).
Frozen Sets: frozenset: immutable version of set. Can be used as dictionary key or as set element (regular sets can't).
fs = frozenset([1,2,3]). All set operations work on frozenset. Cannot add/remove elements. Useful when you need an immutable set.
Performance Analysis of Sets: Set membership check is O(1) average using hash table. List membership check is O(n) — checks every element.
For 1 million items: set in check takes ~0.0001ms. List in check takes ~50ms. Use set when you do frequent membership testing.
List vs Set: Use list when: order matters, duplicates needed, indexing required, small data. Use set when: unique elements needed, fast membership testing, mathematical set operations.
Tuple vs Set: Tuple: ordered, immutable, allows duplicates, indexable. Set: unordered, mutable, no duplicates, not indexable.
Use tuple for ordered fixed data (coordinates, records). Use set for unique collections and fast lookups.

topics.required_code:
# Set comprehension
squares = {x**2 for x in range(-3, 4)}
print(squares)  # {0, 1, 4, 9} — no duplicate 9

evens = {x for x in range(10) if x % 2 == 0}
print(evens)    # {0, 2, 4, 6, 8}

# Frozenset
fs = frozenset([1, 2, 3])
print(fs)  # frozenset({1, 2, 3})
# fs.add(4)  # AttributeError — immutable!

# Use as dict key (regular set can't)
my_dict = {frozenset([1,2]): 'pair'}
print(my_dict[frozenset([1,2])])  # pair

# Performance demo
import time
data = list(range(1_000_000))
data_set = set(data)
target = 999_999
# set lookup is instant, list lookup scans all elements
required_tables: Comparison: Feature | List | Set | Tuple || Order | Maintained | Not maintained | Maintained || Duplicates | Yes | No | Yes || Mutable | Yes | Yes | No || Indexing | Yes | No | Yes || Lookup speed | O(n) | O(1) | O(n) || Use case | Ordered data | Unique+fast lookup | Fixed data
common_mistakes: Using list when set is better for membership testing — if item in large_list is O(n), same with set is O(1)

topics.name: Creating and Accessing Dictionaries

Creating Dictionaries: Dictionary: key-value pairs. Keys must be unique and immutable (strings, numbers, tuples). Values can be anything.
Create: d = {'name':'Alice', 'age':20} or dict(name='Alice', age=20) or {}. Keys maintain insertion order (Python 3.7+).
Adding Elements: Add new key: d['city'] = 'Delhi'. If key exists, value is updated. No separate update method needed.
setdefault(key, default): adds key with default value ONLY if key doesn't exist. Won't overwrite existing values.
Accessing Values (get() method): d['name']: direct access. Raises KeyError if key doesn't exist.
d.get('name'): returns value or None (no error). d.get('salary', 0): returns 0 if key missing. Safer than [] for unknown keys.

topics.required_code:
# Creating
student = {'name': 'Alice', 'age': 20, 'grade': 'A'}
empty = {}
also_dict = dict(name='Bob', age=25)

# Adding/Updating
student['city'] = 'Delhi'    # add new key
student['age'] = 21          # update existing key

# Accessing
print(student['name'])          # Alice
# print(student['salary'])      # KeyError!
print(student.get('salary'))    # None (safe)
print(student.get('salary', 0)) # 0 (default value)

# setdefault
student.setdefault('grade', 'B')  # won't change 'A'
print(student['grade'])            # A (unchanged)
common_mistakes: Using [] to access missing key — KeyError. Always use .get() when key might not exist
Assuming dictionary is unordered — Python 3.7+ dicts maintain insertion order

topics.name: Modifying and Iterating Dictionaries

Pop, Popitem, del keyword, Clear: pop(key): removes key and returns its value. pop(key, default): returns default if key missing (no error).
popitem(): removes and returns last inserted (key, value) pair. del d['key']: removes key (KeyError if missing). d.clear(): removes everything.
Keys, Values, Items: d.keys(): returns all keys as dict_keys view. d.values(): returns all values. d.items(): returns (key,value) pairs.
These are view objects — reflect changes to dict. Convert to list: list(d.keys()). Iterate: for key in d.keys() or simply for key in d.
Using update(): d.update({'key': value}): adds/updates multiple key-value pairs at once. Existing keys overwritten, new keys added.
d.update(d2): merges d2 into d. d2's values win for duplicate keys.
Using ** operator: merged = {**d1, **d2}: creates new merged dictionary. d2 values override d1 for duplicate keys.
Python 3.9+: merged = d1 | d2 (pipe operator). merged = d1.copy(); merged.update(d2) also works.
Using For Loop: Dict comprehension: {key_expr: value_expr for item in iterable}. Squares: {x: x**2 for x in range(1,6)} → {1:1, 2:4, 3:9, 4:16, 5:25}.
Single If Condition: {k: v for k, v in d.items() if v > 10} — keep only entries where value > 10.
Multiple If Conditions: {k: v for k, v in d.items() if v > 10 if v < 50} — value between 10 and 50.
If-Else Condition: {k: 'high' if v > 50 else 'low' for k, v in scores.items()} — categorize values.

common_mistakes: Iterating dict while modifying it — causes RuntimeError. Create a copy first: for k in list(d.keys())
Expecting d.keys() to return a list — it returns dict_keys view. Use list(d.keys()) if you need a list

topics.name: Comparing Data Structures
keywords: list vs tuple vs set vs dictionary
subtopics: List vs Tuple vs Set vs Dictionary

List vs Tuple vs Set vs Dictionary: List: use when you need ordered data that changes — shopping cart, student scores in order, sequence of steps.
Tuple: use when data is fixed and shouldn't change — coordinates (x,y), RGB colors (255,0,0), function return values, database records.
Set: use when you need unique items or fast membership testing — unique usernames, removing duplicates from data, finding common items between two groups.
Dictionary: use when you need to map keys to values — student records (roll_no → marks), word frequency count, configuration settings.

required_tables: Comparison: Feature | List | Tuple | Set | Dictionary || Ordered | Yes | Yes | No | Yes(3.7+) || Mutable | Yes | No | Yes | Yes || Duplicates | Yes | Yes | No | Keys:No,Values:Yes || Indexing | Yes | Yes | No | By key || Syntax | [] | () | {} | {k:v} || Use case | Ordered sequence | Fixed data | Unique items | Key-value mapping
common_mistakes: Using list when dictionary is appropriate — looking up student by roll number in list is O(n), in dict is O(1)

topics.name: String Basics and Operations

Creating and Accessing Strings: String: sequence of characters. Single quotes, double quotes, triple quotes — all valid. Triple quotes for multi-line.
Strings are immutable — s[0] = 'X' gives TypeError. To 'change' a string, create a new one.
Internal Implementation: Python strings are Unicode by default — support all languages including Hindi, Arabic, emoji. Each character is a Unicode code point.
String interning: Python caches small strings — 'hello' is 'hello' may be True (same object). Implementation detail, not something to rely on.
String Formatting (%, format(), join()): % formatting (old): 'Hello %s, you are %d years old' % ('Alice', 20). .format() (modern): 'Hello {}, you are {} old'.format('Alice', 20).
f-strings (best, Python 3.6+): f'Hello {name}, you are {age} old'. Fastest and most readable. Can include expressions: f'{2**8}'.
join(): 'separator'.join(list). ', '.join(['Alice','Bob','Carol']) → 'Alice, Bob, Carol'. Much faster than concatenation with + in a loop.
Built-in String Methods: Case: upper(), lower(), title(), capitalize(), swapcase(). Check: startswith(), endswith(), isdigit(), isalpha(), isalnum().
Clean: strip(), lstrip(), rstrip() — remove whitespace. find(sub): first index of substring (-1 if not found). count(sub): count occurrences.
Transform: replace(old, new), split(sep): string to list. join(list): list to string. center(width), ljust(), rjust().
String Indexing and Slicing: Same as list: s[0] = first char, s[-1] = last char. Slicing: s[start:stop:step]. s[::-1] reverses string.
Strings are immutable — slicing creates new string.
String Concatenation: + operator: 'Hello' + ' ' + 'World'. Creates new string each time — slow in loops.
For building strings in loop, use list then join: parts = []; for item in data: parts.append(str(item)); result = ', '.join(parts). Much faster.

topics.required_code:
# String basics
name = 'Alice'
print(name[0])     # A
print(name[-1])    # e
print(name[1:4])   # lic
print(name[::-1])  # ecilA (reversed)

# Methods
s = '  Hello, World!  '
print(s.strip())          # 'Hello, World!'
print(s.lower())          # '  hello, world!  '
print(s.replace('o','0')) # '  Hell0, W0rld!  '
print(s.split(','))       # ['  Hello', ' World!  ']
print(s.find('World'))    # 9

# Formatting
name, age = 'Alice', 20
print(f'Name: {name}, Age: {age}')       # f-string (best)
print('{} is {} years old'.format(name, age))

# Join (faster than + in loop)
words = ['Python', 'is', 'awesome']
sentence = ' '.join(words)
print(sentence)  # Python is awesome
common_mistakes: Concatenating strings with + in a loop — very slow for large data. Use list + join() pattern instead
find() returns -1 when not found, not None or False — if s.find('xyz') returns -1, not None

topics.name: Regular Expressions

Match, Search, Findall: re.match(pattern, string): matches only at START of string. Returns match object or None.
re.search(pattern, string): searches ANYWHERE in string. Returns first match or None. More commonly used than match().
re.findall(pattern, string): returns LIST of all matches. Empty list if none found. Most commonly used function.
Sub, Split, Compile: re.sub(pattern, replacement, string): replaces all matches with replacement. re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', text) collapses multiple spaces.
re.split(pattern, string): splits string at each match. re.split(r'[,;]', 'a,b;c') → ['a','b','c'].
re.compile(pattern): pre-compiles pattern — use when same pattern used many times. Faster in loops.
Quantifiers and Character Classes: Character classes: \d (digit 0-9), \w (word char: letter/digit/underscore), \s (whitespace), . (any char). Uppercase inverts: \D (non-digit), \W (non-word).
Quantifiers: * (0 or more), + (1 or more), ? (0 or 1), {n} (exactly n), {n,m} (between n and m). \d+ matches one or more digits.
Flags: re.IGNORECASE (re.I): case-insensitive matching. re.search('hello', 'HELLO', re.I) matches.
re.MULTILINE (re.M): ^ and $ match start/end of each line, not just whole string. re.DOTALL (re.S): dot . matches newline too.

topics.required_code:
import re

text = 'My phone is 9876543210 and email is alice@gmail.com'

# findall — most common
phones = re.findall(r'\d{10}', text)
print(phones)  # ['9876543210']

# search — anywhere in string
email_match = re.search(r'[\w.]+@[\w.]+', text)
if email_match:
    print(email_match.group())  # alice@gmail.com

# sub — replace
clean = re.sub(r'\s+', ' ', 'Hello   World  Python')
print(clean)  # Hello World Python

# Character classes and quantifiers
password = 'Hello@123'
strong = bool(re.search(r'(?=.*[A-Z])(?=.*\d)(?=.*[@#$])', password))
print('Strong:', strong)  # True

# Flags
re.findall('hello', 'Hello HELLO hello', re.I)  # ['Hello','HELLO','hello']
common_mistakes: Using re.match() expecting it to search anywhere — match() only checks beginning. Use re.search() to find pattern anywhere in string
Forgetting r prefix for raw strings — r'\d+' is correct. '\d+' treats \d as escape sequence (may cause issues)

topics.name: Exception Handling

Exception Hierarchy: All exceptions inherit from BaseException. Most inherit from Exception. Common built-ins: ValueError (wrong value), TypeError (wrong type), IndexError (bad index), KeyError (missing dict key), ZeroDivisionError, FileNotFoundError.
Hierarchy matters: catching Exception catches most exceptions. Catching ValueError only catches value errors. More specific except clauses should come first.
Try-Except Blocks: try: code that might fail. except: what to do when it fails. finally: always runs (cleanup). else: runs only if no exception occurred.
Without exception handling: one error crashes entire program. With it: program can recover gracefully and show helpful error messages to user.
Grouping Multiple Exceptions: Catch multiple exceptions with tuple: except (TypeError, ValueError) as e:. Or separate except blocks for different handling.
Order matters: specific exceptions first, general (Exception) last. Except Exception catches almost everything — use as last resort.
Using raise Keyword: raise ExceptionType('message'): manually raise an exception. Use when input is logically invalid even if no Python error would occur.
Example: def set_age(age): if age < 0: raise ValueError('Age cannot be negative')
Re-throwing Exceptions: In except block, bare raise (without arguments) re-raises the current exception. Use when you want to log the error but still propagate it.
or raise after some handling to pass exception to the caller.
Custom Exception Handling: Create custom exception by inheriting from Exception. class InvalidAgeError(Exception): pass.
Add custom message and attributes for more informative errors. Useful in larger applications to distinguish your errors from Python's built-in ones.

common_mistakes: Bare except: without exception type — catches even KeyboardInterrupt (Ctrl+C) and SystemExit. Always specify exception type
Putting too much code in try block — only put the risky part in try. Easier to know which line caused the exception

topics.name: File Operations
subtopics: File Modes (Read, Write, Append, Create, Erase)

Reading and Writing Files: open(filename, mode): opens file, returns file object. Always close() after use — or Python keeps file locked.
Basic pattern: f = open('file.txt', 'r'); content = f.read(); f.close(). Better pattern: use with statement (auto-closes).
File Modes (Read, Write, Append, Create,: 'r': read only (default). 'w': write — creates file if not exists, ERASES content if exists. 'a': append — adds to end, doesn't erase.
'x': create new file — error if already exists. 'r+': read and write. 'rb'/'wb': binary mode (for images, PDFs, etc.).
Context Managers (with Statement): with open('file.txt', 'r') as f: — automatically closes file when block ends, even if exception occurs.
Best practice: always use with statement. Prevents file leaks and data corruption from unclosed files.
Reading Techniques (read(), readline(),: read(): reads entire file as one string. read(n): reads n characters. For small files only — large files use too much memory.
readline(): reads one line at a time — efficient for large files. readlines(): reads all lines, returns list of strings (each with \n at end).
Iteration (best for large files): for line in f: process line. Reads one line at a time without loading whole file.
Cursor Position (tell(), seek()): tell(): returns current cursor position (byte offset from start). After reading 5 chars, tell() = 5.
seek(offset, from): moves cursor. seek(0) goes to start. seek(0, 2) goes to end. Useful to re-read file without closing/reopening.
File-to-File Transfer: Read from one file, write to another. with open('source.txt') as src, open('dest.txt', 'w') as dst: dst.write(src.read()).
For large files, read/write in chunks: while chunk := src.read(4096): dst.write(chunk).

topics.required_code:
# Write to file
with open('students.txt', 'w') as f:
    f.write('Alice 85\n')
    f.write('Bob 72\n')
    f.write('Carol 91\n')

# Read entire file
with open('students.txt', 'r') as f:
    content = f.read()
    print(content)

# Read line by line (memory efficient)
with open('students.txt', 'r') as f:
    for line in f:
        print(line.strip())  # strip removes \n

# Read all lines as list
with open('students.txt', 'r') as f:
    lines = f.readlines()  # ['Alice 85\n', 'Bob 72\n', ...]

# Append to file
with open('students.txt', 'a') as f:
    f.write('Dave 78\n')  # adds to end, existing data safe

# tell and seek
with open('students.txt', 'r') as f:
    print(f.read(5))   # reads 5 chars
    print(f.tell())    # 5 (cursor position)
    f.seek(0)          # go back to start
    print(f.read(5))   # reads same 5 chars again
common_mistakes: Opening with 'w' thinking it appends — 'w' ERASES existing content. Use 'a' for appending
Forgetting to close file — causes data corruption/file locks. Always use with statement

topics.name: Introduction to OOP

Classes and Objects: Class: blueprint/template for creating objects. Object: instance created from class — real thing that exists in memory.
class Dog: defines what a dog has (attributes: name, breed) and can do (methods: bark, fetch). Fido = Dog('Fido', 'Lab') creates a Dog object.
__init__(self, ...): constructor — automatically called when object is created. self: reference to the current object — must be first parameter of every method.
Accessing: object.attribute and object.method(). Each object has its own copy of attributes but shares methods with all instances.

topics.required_code:
class Student:
    def __init__(self, name, age, grade):
        self.name  = name   # instance attributes
        self.age   = age
        self.grade = grade
    
    def introduce(self):
        return f'I am {self.name}, age {self.age}, grade {self.grade}'
    
    def study(self, subject):
        return f'{self.name} is studying {subject}'

# Creating objects
alice = Student('Alice', 20, 'A')
bob   = Student('Bob', 22, 'B')

# Accessing
print(alice.name)           # Alice
print(alice.introduce())    # I am Alice, age 20, grade A
print(bob.study('Python'))  # Bob is studying Python

# Each object has own attributes
alice.age = 21  # only changes alice's age, not bob's
print(alice.age)  # 21
print(bob.age)    # 22
common_mistakes: Forgetting self as first parameter — def introduce(): gives TypeError when called. Always include self
Confusing class and object — class is the blueprint, object is the actual instance. Student is class, alice is object

topics.name: OOP Principles

Encapsulation: Encapsulation: bundling data and methods together, hiding internal details. Use _ (protected) or __ (private) prefix to indicate restricted access.
Private attribute: self.__balance — name mangled to _ClassName__balance, prevents accidental modification. Provide getter/setter methods for controlled access.
Polymorphism: Polymorphism: same method name, different behavior in different classes. area() in Circle calculates πr², area() in Rectangle calculates length×width — same name, different logic.
Python supports polymorphism naturally — no interface or abstract class required. If object has the method, it works (duck typing: 'if it walks like a duck...').
Inheritance: Inheritance: child class inherits attributes and methods from parent class. Use class Child(Parent):. Avoids code duplication.
super().__init__() calls parent's constructor. Child can override (redefine) parent methods. isinstance(obj, Parent) returns True for child objects too.
Abstraction: Abstraction: hide complex implementation, show only essential features. User calls calculate_tax() without knowing the formula inside.
Abstract classes (abc module): define methods that MUST be implemented by subclasses. Prevents instantiation of incomplete classes.

common_mistakes: Thinking __ makes attribute truly private — Python uses name mangling but it's accessible as _ClassName__attr. It's a convention, not enforced security
Forgetting super().__init__() in child class — parent's attributes won't be initialized, causing AttributeError

topics.name: Advanced OOP Concepts

Instance and Static Variables: Instance variable: defined with self.variable — each object has its own copy. self.name = name in __init__.
Class variable: defined directly in class body (not in method) — shared by ALL instances. Use for data that should be same for all objects (school_name, species, count).
Static method: @staticmethod decorator — doesn't take self or cls. Like a regular function inside class. Use for utility functions related to class.
Special Methods (__init__(), __new__()): __init__(self): initializer — runs AFTER object is created. Sets up instance attributes. Most commonly used special method.
__new__(cls): constructor — creates the actual object, runs BEFORE __init__. Rarely overridden except for singletons or immutable types.
Other useful special methods: __str__ (string representation, used by print), __len__ (len() function), __add__ (+ operator), __eq__ (== comparison).

topics.required_code:
class Student:
    school = 'ABC College'   # class variable — shared
    count = 0               # counts all students
    
    def __init__(self, name, grade):
        self.name = name     # instance variable — unique
        self.grade = grade
        Student.count += 1  # update class variable
    
    def __str__(self):       # used by print()
        return f'Student({self.name}, {self.grade})'
    
    def __eq__(self, other):  # used by ==
        return self.name == other.name
    
    @staticmethod
    def is_passing(grade):    # utility — no self needed
        return grade >= 60

s1 = Student('Alice', 85)
s2 = Student('Bob', 72)

print(s1)                    # Student(Alice, 85) — __str__
print(s1 == s2)              # False — __eq__
print(Student.count)         # 2
print(Student.school)        # ABC College (shared)
print(Student.is_passing(75)) # True (static method)
common_mistakes: Modifying class variable through instance — s1.school = 'XYZ' creates INSTANCE variable, doesn't change class variable. Use Student.school = 'XYZ'
Using __new__ when __init__ is sufficient — override __new__ only for special cases like singletons

topics.name: Iterators

Difference Between Containers and Non-Co: Container: stores elements in memory — list, tuple, dict, set. You can check length, access by index, iterate multiple times.
Non-container/Generator: produces values one at a time, on demand — doesn't store all values in memory. range(), file objects, generators. Can only iterate once.
__iter__() and __next__(): Iterable: object with __iter__() method that returns an iterator. list, str, range are iterables.
Iterator: object with __next__() method that returns next value. Raises StopIteration when exhausted. iter(list) converts iterable to iterator.
for loop automatically calls iter() then repeatedly calls next() — that's how iteration works internally.
Itertools Module (islice(), cycle()): itertools: standard library module with efficient iterator functions. No memory waste — computes values lazily.
islice(iterable, stop): take first n items without loading all. cycle(iterable): repeats iterable infinitely. chain(a, b): combines iterables.

topics.required_code:
# How for loop works internally
fruits = ['apple', 'banana', 'cherry']
it = iter(fruits)           # get iterator
print(next(it))  # apple
print(next(it))  # banana
print(next(it))  # cherry
# next(it)       # StopIteration!

# Custom iterator
class CountDown:
    def __init__(self, start):
        self.current = start
    
    def __iter__(self):
        return self
    
    def __next__(self):
        if self.current <= 0:
            raise StopIteration
        val = self.current
        self.current -= 1
        return val

for num in CountDown(5):
    print(num, end=' ')  # 5 4 3 2 1

# itertools
from itertools import islice, cycle
numbers = range(1_000_000)
first5 = list(islice(numbers, 5))  # [1,2,3,4,5] — no memory waste

cycle_iter = cycle(['A', 'B', 'C'])
for i, val in enumerate(cycle_iter):
    if i >= 7: break
    print(val, end=' ')  # A B C A B C A
common_mistakes: Iterating an exhausted iterator — after going through all items, calling next() only gives StopIteration. Create new iterator to iterate again
Confusing iterable and iterator — list is iterable (has __iter__), iter(list) returns an iterator (has __next__)

topics.name: Generators
subtopics: Control Flow in Iterators and Generators

yield Keyword: Generator function: uses yield instead of return. When called, returns a generator object — doesn't execute function body immediately.
Each call to next() runs until next yield, pauses, and resumes from same point next time. Function 'remembers' its state between calls.
Memory efficient: a generator that yields 1 million numbers uses almost no memory — computes one at a time. Equivalent list would need hundreds of MB.
Control Flow in Iterators and Generators: Generator expression: (expression for item in iterable) — like list comprehension but with () and lazy evaluation.
sum(x**2 for x in range(1000000)) — computes sum without creating list. send() method: sends value INTO generator. throw() method: sends exception into generator.

topics.required_code:
# Generator function
def count_up(start, end):
    current = start
    while current <= end:
        yield current        # pauses here, returns value
        current += 1         # resumes from here next call

gen = count_up(1, 5)
print(next(gen))  # 1
print(next(gen))  # 2
for num in count_up(1, 5):  # can iterate
    print(num, end=' ')     # 1 2 3 4 5

# Memory comparison
import sys
normal_list = [x**2 for x in range(10000)]   # list
generator   = (x**2 for x in range(10000))    # generator
print(sys.getsizeof(normal_list))  # ~87624 bytes
print(sys.getsizeof(generator))    # 200 bytes!

# Practical: read large file efficiently
def read_large_file(filename):
    with open(filename) as f:
        for line in f:
            yield line.strip()  # one line at a time
# No matter how large the file, memory usage stays constant
common_mistakes: Confusing generator and list comprehension — (x for x in lst) is generator (lazy), [x for x in lst] is list (eager). Use generator for large data
Trying to access generator by index — gen[0] gives TypeError. Generators are forward-only iterators

topics.name: Numpy Library

Understanding arrays and vectorized oper: NumPy array (ndarray): like Python list but much faster for math. All elements same type (homogeneous). Stored contiguously in memory.
Vectorized operation: apply operation to all elements without a loop. np.array([1,2,3]) * 2 → [2,4,6]. 100x faster than Python loop for large arrays.
Creating and manipulating NumPy arrays: np.array([1,2,3]): from list. np.zeros((3,4)): 3×4 array of zeros. np.ones((2,3)): ones. np.arange(0,10,2): like range() but returns array.
np.linspace(0,1,5): 5 evenly spaced values from 0 to 1. np.random.rand(3,3): random values. array.shape, array.dtype, array.ndim, array.size.
Broadcasting and universal functions: Broadcasting: NumPy can do operations on arrays of different shapes when dimensions are compatible. a + b where a is (3,1) and b is (1,3) → result is (3,3).
Universal functions (ufunc): np.sqrt(), np.exp(), np.sin(), np.log() — apply to each element. Much faster than Python loops.
Indexing, slicing, and iterating: 1D: arr[2], arr[1:4], arr[::2]. 2D: arr[row, col], arr[0:2, 1:3]. Boolean indexing: arr[arr > 5] — selects elements where condition is True.
Fancy indexing: arr[[0,2,4]] selects rows 0,2,4. Iterating 2D: for row in arr2d: or iterate elements with np.nditer(arr).
Aggregation functions and statistics: arr.sum(), arr.mean(), arr.std(), arr.min(), arr.max(), arr.median(). Axis parameter: arr.sum(axis=0) sums each column, axis=1 sums each row.
np.percentile(arr, 75), np.corrcoef(x,y) — correlation. These are foundation of data analysis.
Linear algebra with NumPy: np.dot(A, B) or A @ B: matrix multiplication. np.linalg.inv(A): inverse. np.linalg.det(A): determinant. np.linalg.eig(A): eigenvalues and eigenvectors.
Essential for machine learning — neural networks are matrix multiplications at their core.

topics.required_code:
import numpy as np

# Creating arrays
arr = np.array([1, 2, 3, 4, 5])
matrix = np.array([[1,2,3],[4,5,6]])
zeros = np.zeros((3, 3))
print(arr.shape)    # (5,)
print(matrix.shape) # (2, 3)

# Vectorized operations — no loop needed!
print(arr * 2)         # [2 4 6 8 10]
print(arr ** 2)        # [1 4 9 16 25]
print(np.sqrt(arr))    # [1.  1.41 1.73 2.  2.24]

# Indexing
print(matrix[0, 2])    # 3 (row 0, col 2)
print(matrix[:, 1])    # [2, 5] (all rows, col 1)
print(arr[arr > 3])    # [4, 5] (boolean indexing)

# Statistics
data = np.array([23, 45, 12, 67, 34, 56, 78])
print(f'Mean: {data.mean():.1f}')  # Mean: 45.0
print(f'Std:  {data.std():.1f}')   # Std: 21.6
print(f'Max:  {data.max()}')       # Max: 78
common_mistakes: Using Python list operations on NumPy arrays — [1,2,3] + [4,5,6] concatenates, np.array([1,2,3]) + np.array([4,5,6]) adds element-wise
Forgetting that NumPy slices are VIEWS not copies — modifying arr[1:3] modifies original. Use .copy() for independent copy

topics.name: Pandas Library
subtopics: Series and DataFrames: creation, indexing, and selection

Series and DataFrames: creation, indexin: Series: 1D labeled array. Like a column in Excel. DataFrame: 2D table with rows and columns — this is what you work with for data analysis.
Select column: df['name'] or df.name. Select row: df.loc['label'] (by label) or df.iloc[0] (by position). Select cell: df.loc['row','col'].
Multiple columns: df[['name','age']]. Slice rows: df.iloc[0:5]. Conditional select: df[df['age'] > 20].
Data cleaning (handling missing data, du: Check missing: df.isnull().sum() — count NaN per column. df.isnull() → boolean DataFrame.
Handle missing: df.dropna() removes rows with any NaN. df.fillna(0) fills NaN with 0. df['col'].fillna(df['col'].mean()) fills with mean — common for numeric data.
Duplicates: df.duplicated() checks. df.drop_duplicates() removes. df.drop_duplicates(subset=['name']) checks only name column.
Filtering and sorting datasets: Filter rows: df[df['score'] >= 60]. Multiple conditions: df[(df['age'] > 20) & (df['score'] >= 60)] — use & not 'and'.
Sort: df.sort_values('score') ascending. df.sort_values('score', ascending=False) descending. df.sort_values(['dept','score']) multi-column sort.
Merging, joining, and concatenating data: pd.concat([df1, df2]): stack DataFrames vertically (same columns). pd.concat([df1, df2], axis=1): stack horizontally (same rows).
pd.merge(df1, df2, on='id'): like SQL JOIN. how='inner' (default), 'left', 'right', 'outer'. df1.join(df2): join on index.
GroupBy operations and aggregations: df.groupby('department'): splits data by department. Then apply aggregation: .mean(), .sum(), .count(), .max(), .min().
df.groupby('dept')['salary'].mean() — average salary per department. .agg({'salary':'mean','age':'max'}) — different aggregation per column.
Data import/export (CSV, Excel, SQL, etc: Read: pd.read_csv('file.csv'), pd.read_excel('file.xlsx', sheet_name='Sheet1'), pd.read_sql(query, connection).

topics.required_code:
import pandas as pd
import numpy as np

# Create DataFrame
data = {
    'name':  ['Alice', 'Bob', 'Carol', 'Dave', 'Eve'],
    'age':   [25, 30, 28, 35, np.nan],
    'score': [85, 72, 91, 68, 88]
}
df = pd.DataFrame(data)

# Basic info
print(df.shape)         # (5, 3)
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.describe())    # statistics summary

# Handling missing data
print(df.isnull().sum())          # age has 1 NaN
df['age'].fillna(df['age'].mean(), inplace=True)  # fill with mean

# Filtering
passed = df[df['score'] >= 75]
print(passed)

# GroupBy
# If we had a 'dept' column:
# avg_score = df.groupby('dept')['score'].mean()

# Read/Write CSV
# df.to_csv('students.csv', index=False)
# df_loaded = pd.read_csv('students.csv')
common_mistakes: Using 'and' instead of & for multiple conditions — df[df.age > 20 and df.score > 60] gives ValueError. Must use & with parentheses
df[col] = value on a slice gives SettingWithCopyWarning. Use df.loc[condition, col] = value for assignment

topics.name: Matplotlib Library
subtopics: Plotting line graphs, bar charts, histograms, and scatter plots

Plotting line graphs, bar charts, histog: plt.plot(x, y): line graph — for trends over time. plt.bar(x, height): bar chart — for comparing categories. plt.scatter(x, y): scatter plot — for relationship between two variables.
plt.hist(data, bins=10): histogram — for distribution of single variable. plt.pie(values, labels=labels): pie chart — for proportions.
Customizing plots (legends, labels, tick: plt.title('My Chart'), plt.xlabel('X axis'), plt.ylabel('Y axis'): axis labels are essential — always add them.
plt.legend(): shows legend when multiple data series. plt.grid(True): adds gridlines. plt.xticks(rotation=45): rotate tick labels.
Subplots and multiple plots: fig, axes = plt.subplots(rows, cols): creates grid of subplots. axes[0,0].plot(x,y) — plot in first subplot.
plt.figure(figsize=(10,6)): set figure size in inches. figsize=(width, height). Bigger figure for more detail.
Saving plots to files: plt.savefig('chart.png', dpi=300, bbox_inches='tight'): save as PNG. dpi=300 for high quality (print). bbox_inches='tight' prevents labels from being cut off.
Formats: PNG (web), PDF (publication), SVG (vector, scalable). plt.show() displays interactively (in Jupyter/IDE).
Plot styling and aesthetics: plt.style.use('seaborn') or 'ggplot', 'dark_background': apply complete style. color='red', linewidth=2, linestyle='--', marker='o': customize individual elements.
Color: named ('red'), hex ('#FF0000'), RGB tuple ((1,0,0)). Always use colorblind-friendly palettes for professional work.
Visualizing distributions (histograms, K: Histogram: plt.hist(data, bins=20, edgecolor='black'). bins controls granularity — too few hides detail, too many is noisy.
KDE (Kernel Density Estimation): smooth version of histogram. Use seaborn: sns.kdeplot(data). Or plt.hist(data, density=True) + KDE overlay.

topics.required_code:
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import numpy as np

# Line plot
x = np.linspace(0, 10, 100)
y = np.sin(x)
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 4))
plt.plot(x, y, color='blue', linewidth=2, label='sin(x)')
plt.title('Sine Wave')
plt.xlabel('x')
plt.ylabel('y')
plt.legend()
plt.grid(True)
plt.savefig('sine.png', dpi=150)
plt.show()

# Bar chart
categories = ['Math', 'Science', 'English', 'History']
scores = [85, 92, 78, 88]
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
plt.bar(categories, scores, color=['blue','green','orange','red'])
plt.title('Student Scores by Subject')
plt.ylabel('Score')
for i, v in enumerate(scores):  # add value labels
    plt.text(i, v+1, str(v), ha='center')
plt.show()

# Histogram
data = np.random.normal(50, 15, 1000)
plt.hist(data, bins=30, edgecolor='black', color='steelblue')
plt.title('Score Distribution')
plt.xlabel('Score')
plt.ylabel('Frequency')
plt.show()

# Subplots
fig, (ax1, ax2) = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(12, 4))
ax1.plot(x, np.sin(x))
ax1.set_title('Sine')
ax2.plot(x, np.cos(x), color='red')
ax2.set_title('Cosine')
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()
common_mistakes: Forgetting plt.show() — plot created but nothing appears on screen. In Jupyter Notebook it auto-shows, in scripts you need plt.show()
Not calling plt.figure() before new plot — previous plot's elements may bleed into new one

topics.name: Seaborn Library
subtopics: Themes and color palettes for enhanced readability

Categorical plots (boxplot, violin plot,: Seaborn is built on Matplotlib — higher-level API for statistical visualizations. Works beautifully with Pandas DataFrames.
sns.boxplot(x='category', y='value', data=df): shows median, quartiles, outliers per category. sns.violinplot(): shows full distribution shape. sns.swarmplot(): shows individual data points.
Heatmaps and correlation matrices: sns.heatmap(data, annot=True): visualizes 2D matrix as colored grid. Perfect for correlation matrices.
Correlation matrix: df.corr() → shows how strongly each pair of columns correlates (-1 to +1). Heatmap makes it easy to spot strong correlations visually.
Relational plots (scatter, line with reg: sns.scatterplot(x='height', y='weight', data=df, hue='gender'): scatter with color grouping by category.
sns.regplot(x='x', y='y', data=df): scatter + regression line in one. sns.lmplot(): like regplot but supports facets (subplots per category).
Themes and color palettes for enhanced r: sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid'): clean white background with gridlines — professional look. Styles: darkgrid, whitegrid, dark, white, ticks.
Color palettes: sns.set_palette('husl'), 'Set2', 'coolwarm', 'viridis'. Use colorblind-friendly: 'colorblind' or 'tab10'.
Pair plots and multi-variable visualizat: sns.pairplot(df): creates grid of scatter plots for every pair of numeric columns + histograms on diagonal. Best for quick exploration of multi-variable relationships.
sns.pairplot(df, hue='species'): color-codes by category. Essential first step in exploratory data analysis (EDA).

topics.required_code:
import seaborn as sns
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import pandas as pd

# Load built-in dataset for examples
tips = sns.load_dataset('tips')
iris = sns.load_dataset('iris')

# Set theme
sns.set_theme(style='whitegrid')

# Boxplot
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 5))
sns.boxplot(x='day', y='total_bill', data=tips)
plt.title('Bill Amount by Day')
plt.show()

# Heatmap — correlation matrix
plt.figure(figsize=(8, 6))
corr = iris.drop('species', axis=1).corr()
sns.heatmap(corr, annot=True, cmap='coolwarm', fmt='.2f')
plt.title('Feature Correlation Matrix')
plt.show()

# Scatter with regression
sns.regplot(x='total_bill', y='tip', data=tips)
plt.title('Tip vs Bill Amount')
plt.show()

# Pairplot — explore all relationships
sns.pairplot(iris, hue='species')
plt.show()
common_mistakes: Using Seaborn without setting theme — default Matplotlib look is plain. Always sns.set_theme() at start for professional appearance
Passing list to Seaborn instead of DataFrame — Seaborn functions expect DataFrame with column names. Convert data to DataFrame first
